required.title: [For Student] Domain-specific exemplars at professional or near-professional level

supplementary.title: [For Student] Published criticism in the student's primary domain

competence_markers.description: They produce work that would be recognized as serious by people who work seriously in the relevant domain — not flawless, but clearly the product of real skill, effort, and judgment
They write self-critiques that are honest, specific, and actionable — identifying real weaknesses without defensiveness or self-attack
They participate in peer review productively — giving specific, honest feedback and receiving it without defensiveness
They can distinguish between adequate, good, and excellent in their own work and articulate the specific differences
They demonstrate precision appropriate to the domain — working to the correct tolerance, not approximating
They have taught quality standards to someone less experienced and can articulate what quality means in specific, transferable terms
They hold the standard when unobserved — producing the same quality of work whether or not anyone will evaluate it
Their portfolio shows sustained improvement over time, with honest quarterly assessments that accurately identify trajectories
They seek critique rather than avoiding it — from peers, mentors, and practitioners in their domain
They have produced at least one "signature" piece they are willing to present publicly as representative of their current best

narration_prompts.prompt: What does professional-level quality mean in the domain you care most about?
narration_prompts.detail: Describe it in specific terms. What would someone who works in that field notice about excellent work? How close is your current work to that standard?

narration_prompts.prompt: Write a self-critique of your most recent significant piece of work.
narration_prompts.detail: What is strong? What is weak? What would you change? Be specific — not "it could be better" but exactly what and exactly how.

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a peer review you participated in — either giving or receiving.
narration_prompts.detail: What was the most useful feedback? How did you respond to it (honestly)? What did you learn about quality assessment from the process?
Explain the concept and give an example from your own work. What level of quality does this task require, and how do you know?

narration_prompts.prompt: Have you ever taught a standard to someone else?
narration_prompts.detail: What did you teach? How did articulating it change your own understanding of it?

narration_prompts.prompt: When no one is watching and no grade is attached, do you hold the standard?
narration_prompts.detail: Be honest. Describe a specific recent moment where you were the only enforcer. What did you do?

cross_connections.description: The trades are the domain where precision and tolerance are most tangible and most unforgiving. A measurement is accurate or it is not. A joint fits or it does not. The trade workshop is where IC2.2-C standards are tested against physical reality — and physical reality does not grade on a curve.

Artistic standards at Standing Ground involve the same self-critique, peer review, and pursuit of excellence. The artist who cannot assess their own work honestly will plateau. IC2.2-C gives the framework for continued growth in creative domains.

Technique precision in physical performance is IC2.2-C applied to the body. The athlete who holds form standards when fatigued — when no one is counting reps and the shortcut is invisible — is demonstrating IC2.2-C in physical form.

The credibility to hold others to a standard depends entirely on holding yourself to that standard first. IC2.2-C is the prerequisite for legitimate quality leadership. The leader who demands excellence from others while producing adequate work themselves is a hypocrite, and their team knows it.
IC2.5-C extends IC2.2-C into reputation and legacy — what your quality record becomes over years and decades. The standard you hold now is the reputation you build over time.

Producing work below your capacity when you could have done better is a form of dishonesty — a gap between what you represented and what you delivered. IC2.2-C and IC1.3 converge on the same principle: the person you are in private is the person you actually are.

Self-command is what makes unobserved quality standards possible. The person whose identity is settled does not negotiate with themselves about whether to hold the standard. They hold it because that is who they are.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned to do things right. In Weathering Storms, you learned to define what "right" means, compare your work to exemplars, revise deliberately, and measure your improvement. You developed the habit of asking "Is this actually done?" and answering honestly.

Now the standard changes. You are no longer comparing your work to what is good for someone your age, or good for a student, or good for a beginner. You are learning to produce work that would be recognized as serious by people who do this work seriously — work that meets professional-level standards, that can withstand honest peer review, and that you would be willing to attach your name to in a context where the quality is visible and judgment is real.

This is not about becoming professional at age 14 or 16. It is about developing the internal architecture of someone who produces professional-level quality: the ability to self-critique without defensiveness or collapse, the precision to distinguish between adequate, good, and excellent by specific measurable criteria, the capacity to invite and give honest feedback, and the willingness to hold a standard that no one else is enforcing.

The core question has not changed: **"Is this actually done, or did I stop because I got tired?"** But at Standing Ground, the question has full weight. You know what good looks like. You have exemplars in your mind. You have the skills to close the gap. The only remaining question is whether you will hold yourself to the standard when no one is watching — when the difference between adequate and excellent is visible only to you, and calling it done early would cost you nothing except your own self-respect.

That is the test. Standards at Standing Ground are not imposed. They are chosen. And the person who chooses to hold a high standard for themselves — not because anyone is checking, but because producing work below their capacity is a form of dishonesty — that person operates from a fundamentally different internal architecture than the person who does whatever the minimum is and calls it done.

You are building that architecture now.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Professional-level output** — work produced to a standard that would be taken seriously by practitioners in the relevant domain
2. **Self-critique** — the ability to look at your own work and identify its actual weaknesses, without defensiveness and without collapse
3. **Peer review** — inviting honest assessment of your work from others and providing honest assessment of theirs
4. **Precision and tolerance** — understanding the difference between adequate, good, and excellent at the level of specific, measurable criteria
5. **Teaching standards to others** — you do not fully own a standard until you can explain it, demonstrate it, and help someone else meet it
6. **Standard-holding without enforcement** — maintaining quality when no one is checking, no grade is attached, and the only person who would know the difference is you
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Professional-Level Standards — What They Actually Mean

"Professional-level" does not mean you are a professional. It means you are producing work at a level where the quality is recognizable to people who work in the field. It means someone who builds furniture for a living would look at your shelf and say: "That person knows what they are doing." It means someone who writes for publication would read your essay and say: "This is real writing."

This is a high bar. It is also an achievable one — not for every domain simultaneously, but for the domains you invest serious time in. The student who has spent years writing, revising, studying exemplars, and accepting correction can produce writing at 16 that is better than what most adults produce. The student who has spent years in a trade workshop, held to standards, redoing what is not right, can produce work at 17 that a professional would respect.

**What professional-level means in practice:**

- **Precision.** The work is not approximately right. It is right. The measurement is accurate. The word is the correct word, not a word that is close. The joint is tight, not "close enough." Professional work lives in the details, and the details are attended to.

- **Consistency.** The quality is sustained throughout the work, not just in the parts that were interesting or easy. The essay does not have a strong opening and a weak conclusion. The project does not have tight joints in the front and loose joints in the back. Quality is uniform because attention was uniform.

- **Finish.** The work is complete. Not mostly complete. Not "I ran out of time." Complete. The edges are clean. The conclusion concludes. The surface is finished. The work has been taken to its natural endpoint, not abandoned at a convenient stopping point.

- **Judgment.** Professional-level work shows judgment — decisions made well about what to include and exclude, what to emphasize and subordinate, how to allocate time and effort. A student who spends three hours on a decorative detail while the structural integrity is poor has shown poor judgment. A student who ensures the structure is sound and then refines the finish has shown good judgment.

### Part 2: Self-Critique — Seeing Your Own Work Honestly

Self-critique is the capacity to look at your own work the way a skilled external evaluator would — to see its actual weaknesses without defensiveness, and its actual strengths without false modesty.

This is one of the hardest intellectual skills a person can develop, because it requires you to separate yourself from your work while simultaneously caring deeply about the work. You must be close enough to the work to have invested real effort in it, and distant enough to see it clearly. Most people cannot do this. They either over-identify with the work (and therefore cannot see its weaknesses) or they detach from it (and therefore do not care enough to improve it).

**How to practice self-critique:**

1. **Complete the work first.** Do not critique while you create. Finish the piece, the project, the draft. Then step back.

2. **Create distance.** Time is the best distance-maker. Set the work aside for a day, a week if you can. When you return, you will see things you could not see while you were inside the work.

3. **Apply your criteria.** You defined what "done" looks like before you began. Now assess honestly: does the work meet each criterion? Where does it fall short? Be specific. Write it down.

4. **Pretend you are reviewing someone else's work.** This is a useful trick. If you saw this essay, this shelf, this drawing for the first time — produced by a stranger — what would you notice? What would you praise? What would you critique?

5. **Write the critique.** Not in your head — on paper. Writing forces precision. "The opening is weak" becomes "the opening does not establish the argument until the third sentence, and the first two sentences are filler." The second formulation is useful. The first is not.

6. **Act on it.** The critique is not the goal — improvement is. After writing the self-critique, revise the work. Address the weaknesses you identified. Then critique again. Is it better? Where?

**The emotional dimension:** Self-critique can slide into self-attack if you are not careful. The difference: self-critique says "this paragraph is not working because the evidence does not support the claim" (specific, about the work, actionable). Self-attack says "I am terrible at this and will never be good" (global, about the person, paralyzing). If you find yourself in self-attack territory, stop. Return to specifics. Name one concrete thing that can be improved. Fix it. Then name the next one. The antidote to paralysis is action.

### Part 3: Peer Review — Giving and Receiving Honest Assessment

Peer review is the practice of submitting your work to the honest assessment of someone at or near your level, and providing the same assessment for them.

This is harder than receiving correction from a mentor, for two reasons. First, the evaluator is a peer — someone whose opinion you might dismiss ("what do they know?") or whose criticism you might take more personally ("they are just jealous" or "they do not like me"). Second, you must also give honest assessment, which requires courage — telling someone their work is not good enough is uncomfortable, and most people avoid it.

Both skills are essential. The person who cannot receive peer critique will not improve past the ceiling of their own self-assessment. The person who cannot give honest critique will not develop the evaluative skills that make their own self-assessment accurate.

**How to receive peer review:**

1. **Invite it.** Do not wait for someone to offer criticism. Ask for it. Specifically: "I want you to tell me what is weak about this. Not what is good — I can see what is good. Where does it fail?"

2. **Listen without defending.** The same principle from Weathering Storms applies, but at Standing Ground the expectation is higher. You should be able to hear critique without any defensive response — not suppressing it, but genuinely not feeling it, because you have separated your identity from your output.

3. **Evaluate the critique.** Not all peer critique is accurate. Some of it reflects the reviewer's limitations rather than your work's weaknesses. Part of maturity is learning to distinguish between valid critique and noise. But your default should be to assume the critique is valid until you have a specific, articulable reason to believe otherwise.

4. **Apply what is valid.** And discard what is not, without resentment.

**How to give peer review:**

1. **Be specific.** "I like it" and "I don't like it" are both useless. "The argument in section two does not follow from section one — I think you need a transitional claim" is useful.

2. **Be honest.** Telling someone their work is good when it is not is a betrayal disguised as kindness. If you see a weakness, name it. Specifically, clearly, without cruelty.

3. **Name what works first.** Not as a warmup to the bad news — as genuine assessment. The reviewer who can identify both strengths and weaknesses demonstrates better evaluative skill than the reviewer who only finds flaws.

4. **Offer direction, not just diagnosis.** "This is weak" tells the creator nothing they can act on. "This is weak, and I think the problem is X — what if you tried Y?" gives them a path forward.

### Part 4: Precision and Tolerance — The Spectrum of Quality

At Standing Ground, you must be able to distinguish between adequate, good, and excellent — not as feelings, but as specific, measurable assessments.

**Tolerance** is an engineering term. It refers to the acceptable range of variation in a measurement or specification. A shelf that must be 36 inches long has a tolerance: perhaps +/- 1/16 of an inch. Within that range, it is acceptable. Outside that range, it fails.

This concept applies far beyond engineering.

**In writing:** The tolerance for a casual email is wide — minor errors, imperfect structure, informal tone are all acceptable. The tolerance for a published essay is narrow — every sentence should be necessary, every word precise, every paragraph connected to the argument. Knowing the tolerance for the task at hand is a professional skill. Over-polishing a casual email is wasted effort. Under-polishing a published essay is professional failure.

**In trades:** A rough-framing carpenter works to a tolerance of 1/4 inch. A finish carpenter works to 1/64 inch. A machinist works to thousandths of an inch. Each knows their tolerance and works to it consistently. Knowing the tolerance — and working to it, not beyond it and not short of it — is what separates competent work from amateur work.

**In all domains:** You must develop the judgment to assess: What level of quality does this task require? What is the tolerance? And does my work fall within it? This judgment comes from experience, from studying exemplars, and from honest self-assessment. It cannot be taught in the abstract — it is developed through practice across many tasks at many levels.

### Part 5: Teaching Standards to Others

You do not fully own a standard until you can teach it.

Teaching requires a different kind of understanding than doing. When you do something well, much of your knowledge is implicit — embedded in your muscles, your instincts, your habits. When you teach it, you must make the implicit explicit. You must articulate what you are doing, why you are doing it, and what the learner should look for in their own work. This process of articulation deepens your own understanding.

**How this works in practice:**

- When a younger student in your household or community is learning a skill you have developed, help them. Show them the standard. Point out specific qualities. Inspect their work and give honest, kind feedback.
- When you review a peer's work, you are teaching them your standard — modeling what quality assessment looks like.
- When you write self-critiques, you are teaching yourself — articulating what you know so you can apply it more consistently.

**The test of understanding:** If you cannot explain why something is good or bad in specific terms — if all you can say is "it looks right" or "it feels off" — then your standard is still intuitive rather than articulate. IC2.2-C requires articulate standards: you can name the criteria, explain their importance, and help someone else see what you see.

### Part 6: Holding Standards Without Enforcement

This is the final and most important part of IC2.2-C.

At Stretching Branches, the standard was held by the adult. At Weathering Storms, the standard was transitioning from external to internal, with the mentor still providing accountability. At Standing Ground, the standard must be self-held. No one is checking. No grade is attached. The only person who would know the difference between adequate and excellent is you.

This is where most people fail. Not spectacularly — quietly. They produce work that is fine. Acceptable. Passable. And they stop there, because going further requires effort that no one has demanded and no one will reward. The gap between what they produced and what they could have produced is invisible to everyone except themselves. And over time, they stop seeing it too. The standard drops so gradually that they do not notice it dropping.

**The antidote is identity.** IC1.1-C1 introduced the concept of self-command — acting from settled identity rather than momentary decision. IC2.2-C is self-command applied to output. The person who holds quality standards without enforcement does not do it through willpower. They do it because producing substandard work is incompatible with who they are. It is not a decision made in the moment. It is a standing order — decided once and executed always.

**The operational question:** When you are the only one who will see this work — when the audience is you alone, when the grade does not exist, when the shortcut would save hours and no one would ever know — do you hold the standard? That is IC2.2-C. That is the test. And it is a test you take every day, in every piece of work you produce, for the rest of your life.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: Signature Project [Ongoing]
Produce at least one piece of work per quarter that represents your current best — work you are willing to put your name on publicly, that you would show to someone whose judgment you respect. This is not a school assignment. It is a demonstration of your standard, chosen by you, executed to the highest level you can currently produce.

### Practice 2: Structured Self-Critique [After Every Major Piece]
After completing any significant work, write a self-critique. Minimum 300 words. Name what works and why. Name what is weak and why. Identify what you would change if you had more time. Compare the work to the best exemplar you know in that domain. Be honest — brutally honest, but specific, not global.

### Practice 3: Peer Review Exchange [Monthly]
Exchange work with a peer, fellow student, or colleague at least once a month. Each person writes a formal critique of the other's work: strengths, weaknesses, specific suggestions. Discuss the critiques together. The ability to give and receive this kind of honest assessment is a core Standing Ground skill.

### Practice 4: Portfolio Review [Quarterly]
Every three months, review your entire portfolio. Compare your earliest work to your most recent. Write a 500-word assessment: What has improved? What has plateaued? Where are you strongest? Where are you weakest? What specific skill needs the most work in the next quarter? Share this with your mentor.

### Practice 5: Teach the Standard [Ongoing]
When opportunity arises — with younger students, peers, or in any instructional context — teach your quality standards. Demonstrate what done looks like. Inspect someone else's work and give specific, kind, honest feedback. Articulate why something is good or not yet good.

### Practice 6: The Unobserved Standard Test [Daily]
This is not a formal exercise. It is an awareness practice. Notice the moments when no one is watching. The assignment only you will see. The cleanup no one will inspect. The task where the shortcut is invisible. In those moments, observe your own behavior. Did you hold the standard? Did you produce work at the same level you would if someone were evaluating it? If yes, the standard is internalized. If no, you know where the work remains.

### Practice 7: Mentorship Engagement [Ongoing]
Seek out people who hold higher standards than you do — in whatever domain you are working in. Not to be praised, but to be challenged. Ask them to look at your work. Listen to what they see that you do not yet see. The distance between your eye and theirs is the distance you still need to travel.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### From Coach to Colleague

The Standing Ground student should be driving most of their own quality process. They define criteria. They find exemplars. They revise. They self-critique. Your role shifts from leading this process to responding to it.

**In practice, this means:**

- **Review, do not assign.** The student should be bringing work to you for review, not waiting for you to assign revision. If they are still waiting for you to tell them to revise, the Weathering Storms transition is incomplete.

- **Push beyond comfortable.** The student's self-assessment will tend to settle at the level where they feel competent. Your job is to see the next level — the one they cannot see yet from where they are — and point toward it. "This is good. Here is what would make it excellent." This is not discouragement. It is the gift of a higher horizon.

- **Be honest.** At Standing Ground, you owe the student professional-level honesty. Softening your assessment to protect their feelings does them a disservice they will pay for later. "This is not at the level you are capable of" is hard to hear and necessary to say. Pair it with specifics: what is falling short, and what the next level looks like.

- **Share your own quality struggles.** At Standing Ground, the student benefits enormously from seeing that quality is a lifelong struggle, not a destination. Share the work you are not satisfied with. Share the revision you are doing on your own projects. Share the feedback you received that was hard to hear but made your work better. This humanizes the quality pursuit and prevents the student from thinking that quality mastery means never struggling.

### Facilitating Peer Review

Peer review is a critical Standing Ground practice, and it requires your facilitation — not to manage the content, but to establish and maintain the culture.

**What healthy peer review looks like:**
- Specific, honest, kind. Not harsh for the sake of harshness. Not soft for the sake of comfort. Accurate.
- Focused on the work, not the person. "This section is weak" not "you are weak at this."
- Reciprocal. Both parties give and receive. No one is permanently the critic or permanently the subject.
- Productive. The review results in better work, not just in feelings.

**What to watch for:**
- **Review avoidance.** The student who never submits work for peer review is either afraid of critique or believes they are beyond it. Both are problems. The first requires safety-building. The second requires humility-building.
- **Review as performance.** The student who gives extensive, articulate critique of others' work but cannot accept critique of their own is performing expertise rather than engaging in mutual improvement.
- **Social dynamics corrupting the review.** Friends who only say nice things. Rivals who only find flaws. The review must be about the work, not the relationship. If social dynamics are distorting the feedback, restructure the pairings or intervene in the culture.

### Cultivating Self-Critique

Self-critique is the most important Standing Ground skill because it is the one that continues to function when no mentor, no peer, and no external structure is present. The person who can see their own work honestly will continue improving indefinitely. The person who cannot will plateau at whatever level their self-assessment permits.

**How to develop it:**

- **Require written self-critiques.** Not just "how do you think it went?" in conversation. Written, specific, evidence-based self-assessment. The discipline of writing forces precision and prevents the vagueness that lets the student slide past real weaknesses.

- **Compare your assessment to theirs.** After the student writes a self-critique, share your own assessment of the same work. Where do they agree? Where do they disagree? The disagreements are the most valuable — they reveal blind spots. If the student consistently rates their work higher than you do, their self-assessment is inflated. If they consistently rate it lower, they may be in self-attack territory.

- **Track self-critique accuracy over time.** Is the student's self-assessment becoming more accurate? Are they seeing more of what you see? Are the blind spots shrinking? This is a direct measure of IC2.2-C development.

- **Watch for self-critique collapse.** Self-critique can become self-attack if the student conflates their work with their identity. "This paragraph is weak" is self-critique. "I am a terrible writer" is self-attack. If you see the student sliding into global, identity-level negative self-assessment, intervene. Redirect to specifics. The antidote is always the same: name one specific thing, fix it, move on.

### Managing the Unobserved Standard

The ultimate test of IC2.2-C is whether the student holds quality standards when no one is watching. You cannot directly assess this — by definition, you are not there. But you can assess its indicators:

- **Consistency of output.** Is the quality of work consistent regardless of whether it will be reviewed? If work produced "for the portfolio" is excellent but work produced "just for practice" is sloppy, the standard is externally motivated, not internally held.

- **Honesty in self-report.** When you ask the student about their unobserved work, do they answer honestly? Do they report shortcuts taken? A student who always claims to hold the standard perfectly is either extraordinary or dishonest — and statistically, the latter is more likely.

- **The quality of unsupervised projects.** Work the student does entirely on their own, without your oversight, is the best evidence. Is it at the same level as supervised work? Higher? Lower? The gap between supervised and unsupervised quality tells you how internalized the standard actually is.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Professional-Level Output

**Developing well:** The student's best work would be taken seriously by a practitioner in the relevant field. Not flawless — but clearly the product of someone who knows what they are doing, cares about quality, and has the skill to back up the care.

**Developing slowly:** The work is competent but lacks the precision, judgment, or finish that distinguishes professional-level quality. The student seems satisfied with "good" and is not pushing toward "excellent."

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student been exposed to truly excellent work in their domain? (You cannot aim at a target you cannot see.) Is the student being challenged to produce at a higher level, or has the expectation settled at "good for their age"? Are they getting time and support for the revision that professional-level quality requires?

### 2. Self-Critique Accuracy

**Developing well:** The student's written self-critiques are specific, honest, and closely aligned with external assessment. They see what a skilled reviewer would see — both strengths and weaknesses.

**Developing slowly:** Self-critiques are vague ("it was okay"), inflated ("I think it is really good" when it clearly is not), or self-attacking ("I am terrible at everything"). The student's self-assessment does not match external assessment.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you comparing your assessment to theirs regularly? Are you naming the discrepancies and discussing them? Is the student being taught the vocabulary and framework for precise self-assessment, or is "how do you think it went?" the extent of the practice?

### 3. Peer Review Capacity

**Developing well:** The student gives specific, honest, constructive feedback and receives it without defensiveness. They participate in peer review as a normal part of their quality process, not as an ordeal.

**Developing slowly:** The student avoids peer review, gives only vague or uniformly positive feedback, or becomes defensive when receiving critique from peers.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the peer review culture established? Are there suitable peers to review with? Is the student being coached in how to give effective feedback, or just told to "review each other's work"?

### 4. Precision and Judgment

**Developing well:** The student understands the quality tolerance for different tasks and works to the appropriate level. They do not over-polish trivial work or under-polish significant work. Their judgment about where to invest quality effort is maturing.

**Developing slowly:** The student applies the same level of effort to everything (either too much or too little), showing a lack of judgment about what different tasks require.

### 5. Teaching Standards

**Developing well:** The student can explain quality standards to someone less experienced in specific, transferable terms. They can demonstrate what quality looks like, identify gaps in someone else's work, and give feedback that actually helps.

**Developing slowly:** The student's standards are intuitive — they can produce quality work but cannot articulate why it is quality or help someone else get there.

### 6. Unobserved Standard

**Developing well:** The quality of unsupervised work matches supervised work. The student reports honestly about shortcuts and lapses. The gap between observed and unobserved output is small or nonexistent.

**Developing slowly:** Supervised work is significantly better than unsupervised work. The standard is still externally motivated.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Relaxing Because the Student Is Good
The Standing Ground student produces good work, and the temptation is to stop pushing. This is how good becomes the permanent ceiling. Your job at Standing Ground is to keep pointing at the horizon beyond good — to show the student what excellent looks like and to maintain the expectation that they will pursue it. Comfort is the enemy of growth.

### Confusing Self-Critique With Self-Attack
A student who writes brutal self-critiques may look like they have high standards, but if the critiques are global ("I am terrible"), emotional ("I hate everything I make"), or paralyzing ("I should not even try"), they are in self-attack, not self-critique. Self-critique is precise, actionable, and leads to improvement. Self-attack is vague, emotional, and leads to avoidance. They look similar on the surface. Distinguish them by their fruit: does the self-assessment lead to better work, or to paralysis?

### Becoming the Only Evaluator
If the student only accepts quality feedback from you, they have not developed the capacity for peer review and self-assessment that Standing Ground requires. Deliberately widen the circle: introduce peer review partners, encourage the student to seek critique from practitioners in their domain, and gradually reduce your role as the primary evaluator.

### Neglecting the Identity Dimension
IC2.2-C is not just about producing good work. It is about becoming a person who produces good work as a matter of identity. If you focus only on the output and neglect the identity formation, you will produce a student who can meet standards when pushed but reverts to adequate when unsupervised. The identity conversation matters: "What kind of person do you want to be? What does your work say about that person?"

### Letting the Student Coast on Talent
Some students produce good work without significant effort, because they are talented. This is a dangerous position, because they have never had to push through the gap between what came easily and what requires genuine struggle. When they eventually encounter that gap — and they will — they will not have the quality discipline to survive it. If a student is coasting, raise the standard until they cannot coast. The struggle is where IC2.2-C is actually built.

### Failing to Model Your Own Quality Pursuit
At Standing Ground, "do as I say, not as I do" does not work. If you are asking the student to hold professional-level standards, they need to see you holding standards in your own life. Share your work. Share your revision process. Share the feedback that was hard to hear. Share the project you are not satisfied with yet. The student at this age can detect hypocrisy with precision — and a mentor who preaches quality while accepting mediocrity in their own output has no credibility.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Professional-Level Quality
**Strong:** Specific, domain-literate description of what professional quality means. References real practitioners and real standards. Honest assessment of the gap between their current work and that standard. "In cabinetry, professional-level means joints with no visible gaps, surfaces sanded to 220 grit minimum, and a finish that is even and does not show brush strokes. My joints are getting tight but my finish work is inconsistent."
**Weak:** Vague. "It means doing it really well." No domain-specific criteria. No honest self-assessment of the gap.

### Prompt 2: Self-Critique
**Strong:** The self-critique is specific, balanced (names both strengths and weaknesses), honest, and actionable. The student identifies real weaknesses without spiraling into self-attack, and names specific steps for improvement. Aligns closely with what a skilled external reviewer would say.
**Weak:** Vague ("it was okay"), inflated ("I think it is great"), or self-attacking ("it is terrible and I am terrible"). No specific observations. No actionable next steps.

### Prompt 3: Peer Review Experience
**Strong:** Describes specific feedback given or received. Explains what made it useful. Shows understanding of why peer review is valuable — not just as a requirement but as a tool for seeing what you cannot see alone. "My peer pointed out that my argument assumed something I had not proven. I did not see that because I knew what I meant — but the reader did not."
**Weak:** "We swapped papers and said it was good." No substantive feedback exchanged. No insight into the value of the process.

### Prompt 4: Precision and Tolerance
**Strong:** Explains the concept clearly with a real example from their own work. Demonstrates judgment about appropriate quality levels for different tasks. "My quarterly essay needs to be tight — every paragraph supporting the thesis, every sentence doing work. My daily journal does not need that level of precision. Knowing the difference saves time and produces better work where it matters."
**Weak:** Cannot explain the concept. Does not distinguish between quality levels for different tasks.

### Prompt 5: Teaching Standards
**Strong:** Describes a specific instance of teaching. Explains what articulating the standard taught them about their own understanding. "I was helping a younger student with their woodworking. Trying to explain what a good joint looks like made me realize I had been relying on feel — I could do it but I could not explain it. Now I can explain the three things to check, and my own joints have gotten more consistent because I check them more deliberately."
**Weak:** Has not taught anyone. Or describes teaching as "I told them to do it right" without specific instruction.

### Prompt 6: The Unobserved Standard
**Strong:** Honest. Describes a specific moment. Acknowledges the temptation to cut corners and reports what actually happened. May admit a failure: "Last week I rushed a cleanup because no one would see it. I caught myself and went back. But the fact that I almost let it go tells me the standard is not fully automatic yet." This level of honesty is more advanced than "I always hold the standard."
**Weak:** "I always hold the standard." (Likely dishonest, or the student has not developed the self-awareness to notice the moments where they do not.) Or: cannot describe a specific instance.
